findings.description: Found 1 placeholder that looks like unfilled template content: "[Premises Address]".
findings.explanation: A bracketed placeholder, mustache token, or XXX run that survived into the final document is almost always a drafting accident — the contract is meant to be filled in. A placeholder where a counterparty name belongs makes the agreement legally questionable; a placeholder elsewhere makes the agreement embarrassing.
findings.recommendation: Replace every flagged placeholder with the intended content, or remove the bracket if the content is no longer needed.
findings.title: Unfilled template placeholders: 1

findings.description: Tenant hereby waives any right to trial by jury

excerpt.text: Tenant hereby waives any right to trial by jury in any action arising from this Lease.

findings.explanation: A one-sided jury-trial waiver binds the counterparty to a bench-only or arbitration-only forum while leaving the drafter free to demand a jury. Even where enforceable (most US jurisdictions under the FAA and Seventh Amendment), the asymmetry is a recognized dark-pattern signal — particularly in consumer- and employee-facing contracts. *Leasing Service Corp. v. Crane* (4th Cir. 1986) requires the waiver to be `knowing and voluntary`, which courts apply more strictly to one-sided waivers.
findings.recommendation: Make the waiver bilateral (`each party hereby waives any right to trial by jury`). If the asymmetry is intentional, document the consideration that supports the affected party's `knowing and voluntary` waiver.
findings.title: Asymmetric jury-trial waiver

findings.description: Worker / party is identified as California-based but the contract selects New York law.
findings.explanation: Cal. Lab. Code § 925 voids out-of-state choice-of-law clauses in employment contracts entered into, modified, or extended after Jan. 1, 2017 — unless the employee was individually represented by counsel in negotiating that specific clause. Cal. Bus. & Prof. Code § 16600.5 (2024) extends similar reasoning to non-competes: they are unenforceable regardless of where signed if the worker is in California, and create a private right of action with attorneys' fees. This pattern is commonly used to evade California's strict non-compete prohibitions; courts (e.g., *Lyon v. Neustar*) routinely refuse to enforce it.
findings.recommendation: Either change governing law to California, or include a §925 counsel-representation acknowledgment. Note that §16600.5 may render any non-compete unenforceable even with §925-compliant choice-of-law language.
findings.title: Out-of-state choice-of-law on California-based party

findings.description: Tenant shall provide Landlord at least 60 days written notice of any claim before initiating suit

excerpt.text: Tenant shall provide Landlord at least 60 days written notice of any claim before initiating suit.

findings.explanation: A pre-suit notice or cure window imposed on one party (typically the consumer / customer / employee) but not on the drafter compresses the affected party's ability to act quickly — by the time the notice period expires, evidence has aged and pressure to settle has built. The drafter, meanwhile, can initiate proceedings without warning. Confirm whether the asymmetry is a legitimate dispute-resolution structure (e.g., an MSA's `cure for material breach` provision) or a one-sided friction gate.
findings.recommendation: If the gate is meant as a dispute-cooling mechanism, make it bilateral (`each party shall provide…`). If it is a one-sided cure window for the drafter's benefit, weigh whether the trade is intended.
findings.title: Asymmetric pre-suit notice / cure window

findings.description: The contract specifies a late-payment rate of 3% per month, which annualizes to approximately 36.0%.

excerpt.text: Late Payment: A late fee of 3% per month shall apply to amounts not paid when due.

findings.explanation: Most US states cap consumer interest in the 18–24%/year range; commercial transactions allow higher contractual rates in many states but several (New York General Obligations Law §5-501, Tennessee, Arkansas pre-Constitutional-Amendment) hold the line tighter. A rate that would be standard in Texas or Delaware may be void in New York or California consumer contexts. Confirm against the controlling jurisdiction.
findings.recommendation: Confirm the rate is enforceable under the controlling state's usury rules. For consumer-facing contracts, consider capping at 18% annualized. For commercial contracts, document the parties' acknowledgement of the rate.

source_citations.source: New York General Obligations Law § 5-501 (civil usury)

findings.title: Late-payment rate above 18%/year: ~36.0%

findings.description: Vaulytica did not find a limitation-of-liability clause.
findings.explanation: Without a limitation-of-liability clause, exposure is bounded only by what the parties can prove in damages. Most commercial contracts cap liability.
findings.title: No limitation-of-liability clause detected

findings.description: Indemnification language is present (`shall indemnify`) but no clause caps the aggregate exposure.
findings.explanation: An indemnity carved out of (or simply not subject to) the liability cap can be the largest single financial risk a contract carries. A third-party IP infringement claim, a data-breach notification cost, or a regulatory fine can dwarf the contract value many times over. Confirm the carve-out is deliberate and proportionate to the indemnifying party's solvency.
findings.recommendation: Either subject indemnification to a per-claim and aggregate cap, or confirm the carve-out is a deliberate trade for higher pricing / different insurance.
findings.title: Indemnification without aggregate cap

findings.description: shall maintain commercial general liability insurance

excerpt.text: Tenant shall maintain commercial general liability insurance during the Term.

findings.explanation: A bare `shall maintain insurance` clause is essentially unenforceable: the indemnitee has no way to test whether the counterparty's coverage is adequate, and a $1,000 homeowner policy formally satisfies the clause. Standard commercial drafting names a per-occurrence limit (typically $1M), an aggregate limit (typically $2M), and the coverage type (CGL, professional liability, cyber, etc.).
findings.recommendation: Specify (1) the coverage types required (CGL, professional liability, errors & omissions, cyber, workers' comp, auto), (2) the per-occurrence limit, (3) the aggregate limit, and (4) the certificate-of-insurance / additional-insured cooperation obligations.
findings.title: Insurance requirement without coverage minimum

findings.description: No Effective Date is named, defined, or stated near the top of this Agreement.
findings.explanation: Most contracts identify a starting point that other date references rely on. Without it, relative terms like 'within 30 days after the Effective Date' have no anchor.
findings.recommendation: Add an Effective Date — either in the preamble ('dated as of [date]'), as a defined term, or as an explicit 'Effective Date: [date]' line.
findings.title: No Effective Date found

excerpt.text: Term is three (3) years, with automatic renewal for successive one-year terms unless either party provides written notice of non-renewal at least 10 days before the end of the then-current Term.

findings.explanation: Auto-renewal commits the customer to another term unless they actively opt out. The notice window is the critical detail; verify it is reasonable and well-located.
findings.title: Auto-renewal clause present

excerpt.text: automatic renewal for successive one-year terms unless either party provides written notice of non-renewal at least 10 days

findings.explanation: Typical auto-renewal notice windows are 30 to 90 days. Anything outside that band — too long and the customer is likely to forget, too short and they are likely to miss it — warrants review under FTC negative-option marketing guidance (16 CFR Part 425).
findings.title: Auto-renewal notice window of 10 days is unusual

findings.description: Auto-renewal requires non-renewal notice 10 days in advance.

excerpt.text: The initial Term is three (3) years, with automatic renewal for successive one-year terms unless either party provides written notice of non-renewal at least 10 days before the end of the then-current Term.

findings.explanation: An under-30-day non-renewal window compresses the customer's decision time. The FTC's Negative Option Rule and state-level auto-renewal statutes (California BPC §17600 et seq., New York GBL §527-a, and similar) constrain this in consumer contexts; even where the contract is B2B, short windows are widely reported as a friction-based dark pattern.
findings.recommendation: Negotiate a 30- or 60-day non-renewal notice window.
findings.title: Auto-renewal notice window under 30 days: 10

findings.description: The contract does not state a path to terminate for material breach.
findings.explanation: Without a for-cause termination path, parties must rely on common-law material-breach doctrines, which are jurisdiction-dependent.
findings.title: No termination-for-cause clause detected

findings.description: The contract does not state what happens upon termination.
findings.explanation: An effect-of-termination clause spells out the rights and obligations that survive, the return or destruction of materials, and the wind-down rules.
findings.title: No effect-of-termination clause detected

findings.description: The document does not state where disputes must be brought.
findings.explanation: Without a venue clause, default venue rules apply (in federal court, 28 U.S.C. § 1391). A clear forum-selection clause avoids ambiguity.
findings.title: No venue / forum clause detected

findings.description: waives any right to trial by jury

excerpt.text: Tenant hereby waives any right to trial by jury in any action arising from this Lease.

findings.explanation: Jury waivers are enforceable in most US jurisdictions but unenforceable in some contexts (e.g., California for pre-dispute employment waivers). Confirm enforceability against the governing-law jurisdiction.
findings.title: Jury trial waiver present

findings.description: Only tenant bears this typically-mutual obligation.
findings.explanation: Obligations like confidentiality, indemnity, and representations are usually mutual. A one-sided version is sometimes intentional but worth confirming.
findings.title: Asymmetric indemnification obligation

findings.description: Indemnity clause appears to be missing: notice, defense control, settlement consent.

excerpt.text: Tenant shall indemnify and hold Landlord harmless from any claim arising from Tenant's occupancy of the Premises.

findings.explanation: A complete indemnity clause specifies (a) the timeline and form for notice of a claim, (b) which party controls defense, and (c) whether settlement requires consent.
findings.title: Indemnity procedural elements missing: notice, defense control, settlement consent

playbook_match_reasoning: Selected lease-commercial-multitenant (score 0.9). Title matched: "office lease". Distinguishing phrases: "Landlord", "Tenant", "Premises", "Common Area", "Rentable Square Feet", "Base Rent", "Operating Expenses".
